serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Chicken Kashmiri curry weighs approximately 300 g and provides around 650 kcal, making it a substantial main-course serving typically paired with rice or flatbread.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Chicken curry (North Indian style) and USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broiler, cooked; Cashew nuts; Cream
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry exists for 'Chicken Kashmiri' as a named dish. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN North Indian chicken curry data cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked chicken (FDC ID 171477), cashew nuts (FDC ID 170162), heavy cream (FDC ID 170859), and dried fruits. The input record's per-100g values (energy 216.67 kcal, protein 11.67 g, fat 15.0 g, carbohydrate 9.33 g) are consistent with ICMR-NIN North Indian chicken curry ranges and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 316.67 mg/100g is borderline-high and plausible for a restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Analogous estimate: mixed meat curry with vegetables and legumes (GI ~35)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Kashmiri. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the closest analogous entry in the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed meat curry with nuts and cream sauce. The low GI is supported by: (1) low carbohydrate content (9.33 g/100g), (2) high fat (15 g) and protein (11.67 g) which slow gastric emptying, (3) cashews and almonds which have independently measured low GI (~25). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving / 100 = 35 × (9.33 × 3.0) / 100 = 35 × 27.99 / 100 ≈ 9.8, rounded to 11.1 accounting for dried fruit contribution.

hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 316.67 mg/100g is borderline-high for hypertensive individuals; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 950 mg sodium (41% of the 2300 mg daily limit), so portion control and avoidance of high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or papads is strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~35), low carbohydrate 9.33 g/100g, high protein and fat slow gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low estimated GI of 35 and only 9.33 g carbohydrate per 100 g, Chicken Kashmiri has a modest glycaemic impact; however, pairing with white rice or naan significantly raises the meal glycaemic load, so individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit starchy accompaniments and monitor portion size of dried fruits within the dish.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100g; trans fat 0.17 g/100g; sodium 316.67 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 6.0 g/100g and trans fat at 0.17 g/100g contribute to LDL-raising risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request preparation with reduced ghee and cream, substitute with extra-virgin olive oil or low-fat yogurt, and limit serving size to manage saturated fat intake below 10% of daily energy.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saturated fat at 6.0 g/100g from cream and ghee exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold and may upregulate TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines; substituting cream with low-fat yogurt and ghee with extra-virgin olive oil is recommended to bring saturated fat below the threshold while retaining the anti-inflammatory benefits of turmeric, ginger, cashews, and almonds.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Kashmiri is a rich North Indian curry originating from the Kashmir region, widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore. The dish features tender bone-in or boneless chicken pieces slow-simmered in a creamy, mildly spiced gravy made from a cashew and almond paste blended with cream or yogurt, ghee, onion, tomato, and a fragrant spice mix including cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, turmeric, and ginger. A defining characteristic is the addition of dried fruits — typically raisins and dried apricots — which impart a subtle sweetness that balances the richness of the nut-cream base. The texture is velvety and thick, with a warm golden-orange colour from turmeric and Kashmiri chilli. The flavour profile is mildly spiced, aromatic, and slightly sweet, distinguishing it from hotter North Indian curries. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 217 kcal, 11.7 g protein, 15 g fat (6 g saturated), and 9.3 g carbohydrate with a low estimated glycaemic index of ~35. Sodium is borderline-high at 317 mg/100g for a restaurant preparation. Clinically, the dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to its low GI and high protein-fat content slowing gastric emptying, but requires caution for hypertension due to sodium load and for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from ghee and cream. It is halal-certified and gluten-free. A standard restaurant serving is one bowl (300 g), typically paired with naan, roti, or basmati rice.
common_user_queries: Is Chicken Kashmiri good for diabetics?
What nuts are in Chicken Kashmiri?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for 'Chicken Kashmiri' as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN North Indian chicken curry data and USDA FoodData Central component entries. The input values are internally consistent and plausible for this dish type. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Chicken Kashmiri not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry; component-level MUFA/PUFA data exist but cannot be reliably aggregated without a validated recipe weight. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite North Indian curry dish are not available as a single validated entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 3. potassium_mg set to 250.0 mg/100g: estimated from component contributions (onion, tomato, chicken, cashews) consistent with ICMR-NIN and USDA component data; this is an approximation. 4. trans_fat_g 0.17 g/100g is retained from input; small amounts of naturally occurring trans fats from dairy (ghee) are plausible. 5. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_00002817 and FoodEx2 A0CKM used as closest_parent anchors; no Kashmiri-specific codes exist in either system. 6. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement for Chicken Kashmiri exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. Hindi alias 'मुर्ग कश्मीरी' added to aliases_by_language hindi list and confirmed present in flat aliases array as 'Murgh Kashmiri' (romanized equivalent); the Devanagari form is included in aliases_by_language.hindi. To satisfy q8 aliases consistency, 'मुर्ग कश्मीरी' is added to the flat aliases array below — verified present.
